cuisine: Global / Multi-regional (South Asian, Southeast Asian, Mediterranean)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of mustard seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 3 g), used as a whole spice in tempering, pickling, or seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Mustard seeds, yellow, whole — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02024)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060042, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mustard seeds (FDC ID 170931). Values are consistent between sources. HPB is the primary source of record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mustard Seeds — analogous estimate based on whole seeds with high fibre and fat content; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw mustard seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high dietary fibre content (12.2 g/100g) which substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (2) high fat content (36.24 g/100g) which slows gastric emptying; (3) net carbohydrate of approximately 15.9 g/100g after fibre; (4) analogy with other whole oilseeds (e.g. flaxseed GI ~35, sesame seeds GI ~35) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI x available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 35 x (0.879 g per 3 g serving) / 100 = 0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (13 mg/100g) and very high potassium (738 mg/100g) with a favourable Na:K ratio supporting DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Mustard seeds are an excellent spice choice for hypertensive individuals: sodium is negligible at 13 mg/100g, potassium is very high at 738 mg/100g, and the high fibre content (12.2 g/100g) supports vascular health; use freely as a culinary spice but avoid prepared mustard condiments with added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (12.2 g/100g) reduces net carbohydrate to approximately 15.9 g/100g; glucosinolates may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mustard seeds have a low estimated glycaemic index (~35) and a negligible glycaemic load per teaspoon serving (~0.3); the high fibre content attenuates postprandial glucose response, and bioactive glucosinolates have been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in preclinical studies — use freely as a spice in typical culinary quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid, very low saturated fat (1.99 g/100g), zero trans fat, high fibre, and high selenium with antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mustard seeds provide a cardioprotective fatty acid profile with high polyunsaturated fat (predominantly omega-3 ALA at ~10 g/100g), very low saturated fat (1.99 g/100g), zero trans fat, and high dietary fibre (12.2 g/100g); selenium content (208 mcg/100g) provides antioxidant support — use as a regular culinary spice to support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid, selenium, and glucosinolates with established anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mustard seeds contain anti-inflammatory compounds including omega-3 ALA, selenium, and glucosinolates; saturated fat is very low (1.99 g/100g) and trans fat is zero — use in culinary quantities, particularly whole seeds in tempering (tadka), to retain bioactive anti-inflammatory compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mustard seeds are small, round, hard seeds harvested from the mustard plant (Brassica juncea, Brassica nigra, or Sinapis alba), available in yellow, brown, and black varieties. Raw mustard seeds have a firm texture and a sharp, pungent, slightly bitter taste that mellows and becomes nuttier when heated in oil during tempering (tadka). They are a foundational spice in South Asian, Southeast Asian, Mediterranean, and European cuisines, used whole in hot oil to release their volatile oils, ground into mustard paste or powder, or used in pickling brines. Nutritionally, mustard seeds are exceptionally dense: per 100 g they provide 508 kcal, 26.08 g protein, 36.24 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid), 28.09 g carbohydrate, and 12.2 g dietary fibre. The high fibre content reduces net carbohydrate to approximately 15.9 g/100g and confers a low estimated glycaemic index (~35). Sodium is negligible at 13 mg/100g while potassium is very high at 738 mg/100g, making mustard seeds an outstanding DASH-diet spice. Selenium content is exceptionally high at approximately 208 mcg/100g, providing strong antioxidant support. Bioactive glucosinolates have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and potential insulin-sensitising properties in research. Mustard seeds are a declared allergen in the EU and many jurisdictions. In Singapore and South Asian cooking, mustard seeds are commonly used in curry bases, dal, vegetable stir-fries, and rice dishes. They are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060042, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170931) with consistent results. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence sub-component) based on University of Sydney GI Database oilseed analogues and peer-reviewed literature; overall record confidence remains high given the strong macro and micronutrient provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.99 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (22.52 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (10.09 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170931 to supplement HPB data which does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. 2. Vitamin and mineral micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170931. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 and vitamin D is 0.0: these are naturally zero for a plant-based seed food with no animal-derived components — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0: naturally zero as mustard seeds are a plant food — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources. 5. Trans fat is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw mustard seeds. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for raw mustard seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Estimation basis: high fibre (12.2 g/100g), high fat (36.24 g/100g), net carbohydrate ~15.9 g/100g, and analogy with whole oilseeds (flaxseed, sesame) from approved GI sources. 7. Mustard is a declared allergen in the EU (EU Food Information for Consumers Regulation 1169/2011) and should be flagged in allergen-sensitive contexts. 8. Selenium value of 208.1 mcg/100g from USDA SR Legacy is notably high; this is consistent with published literature for mustard seeds and is retained as reported. 9. No -1 sentinel values require documentation as all fields have been populated from approved sources.
